title: Free to Choose: A Personal Statement
author: Milton Friedman and Rose D. Friedman
subtitle: The Case for Free Markets, Limited Government, and Individual Liberty
description: A personal statement by Nobel laureate Milton Friedman and his wife Rose Friedman, making the case for free markets, limited government, and individual liberty. Originally published as the companion to a 10-part PBS television series, the book explains how voluntary exchange and market competition create prosperity, while government intervention — however well-intentioned — produces unintended harm. Across 10 chapters it tackles inflation, education, consumer protection, occupational licensing, the welfare state, and international trade, offering a coherent free-market alternative to the postwar consensus.
keyIdeas: Voluntary exchange in free markets is the engine of prosperity and the foundation of political freedom
Prices serve three functions: transmitting information, providing incentives, and distributing income
The Great Depression was caused by Fed mismanagement of the money supply, not by capitalism
Government inflation is caused by expanding the money supply faster than output
School vouchers would introduce competition and improve education for all
The welfare state should be replaced with a negative income tax
Occupational licensing should be dramatically reduced
Consumer protection is best ensured by market competition, not regulation
Free trade benefits all parties; tariffs protect special interests at public expense
Equality of opportunity matters; equality of outcome requires coercion
An Economic Bill of Rights would constitutionally limit government spending, taxation, and money creation
There is no such thing as a free lunch — every government program has hidden costs
keyTakeaways: Free markets coordinate the activities of millions of people without central direction through the price system
Government intervention in the economy almost always produces unintended consequences worse than the original problem
Inflation is always and everywhere a monetary phenomenon caused by government printing too much money
School vouchers would empower parents, introduce competition, and improve educational outcomes
A negative income tax would replace the welfare bureaucracy with a simpler, more humane cash transfer system
Occupational licensing restricts opportunity and raises costs for consumers without improving quality
Consumer protection through market competition is more effective than government regulation
International free trade benefits all parties regardless of wage differences
Economic freedom is a necessary condition for political freedom
Constitutional limits on government spending and taxation are needed to preserve liberty
whoShouldRead: Anyone interested in the philosophical and practical case for free markets and limited government
Students of economics seeking a clear, accessible introduction to market-liberal thought
Policymakers and politicians from across the ideological spectrum
Viewers of the original PBS series who want the full argument in book form
Critics of free-market economics who want to understand the strongest version of the opposing case
